title: RMDM Overview
html_body:
Reference Master Data Management (RMDM) is the authoritative governance framework for critical reference and master data across the enterprise. RMDM provides a single source of truth for business entities and their relationships, enabling consistent data-driven decision-making across all business lines and operating systems.

The RMDM model comprises six core entities: Counterparty (legal entities and trading counterparts), ClientAccount (customer accounts with settlement and custody details), Book (trading books and banking books with regulatory boundaries), SettlementInstruction (payment and settlement instructions with SWIFT standards), Country (geopolitical reference data with regulatory overlay), and Currency (monetary denominators with deliverability flags). These entities form an integrated dependency graph where Country and Currency serve as reference dimensions for all transactional entities.

RMDM governance is structured around three distinct roles: Data Owners (executive accountability for data quality), Data Stewards (operational responsibility for data maintenance), and the Data Governance Committee (strategic oversight and policy alignment). Each role operates within defined SLAs that cover data accuracy, completeness, timeliness, and validity. The governance model is underpinned by BCBS 239 principles (Regulatory Risk Assessment on Data Management) and aligns with regulatory frameworks including EMIR, MiFID II, and FATCA requirements.

The system integrates with multiple source systems: Bloomberg for market data, Murex for trading and booking, Summit for risk management, SWIFT for settlement messaging, GBS (Group Banking System) for banking operations, and CPI (Corporate Platform Inc.) for reference data syndication. Each system maintains specific responsibilities in the data lifecycle, with RMDM acting as the authoritative registry and resolution point.

Data quality is managed through a rules-based validation framework comprising mandatory rules (non-negotiable data requirements), conditional rules (requirements that apply under specific conditions), and a severity hierarchy from ERROR (block downstream processing) through WARNING (flag for manual review) to INFO (audit trail). Business rules are version-controlled and traced to regulatory requirements, enabling audit compliance and impact analysis for rule changes.

RMDM deployment follows a cloud-native architecture with microservices, event-driven workflows, and API-first integration patterns. Master data is replicated to operational indices in Elasticsearch for high-performance querying, while the source of truth remains in SQL Server with MongoDB extensions for flexible attribute schemas.

title: Counterparty Data Model
html_body:
Counterparty is the foundational entity in RMDM, representing legal entities that engage in financial transactions as trading counterparts, settlement agents, custodians, or other operational roles. Each counterparty record captures core identity attributes, regulatory status, and operational configuration necessary for transaction processing and compliance screening.

Counterparties follow a standardized lifecycle with four distinct status values: PROSPECT (new entity under evaluation), ACTIVE (cleared for full trading and settlement), SUSPENDED (temporarily restricted due to compliance or operational issues), and TERMINATED (permanently closed, retained for audit trail). The lifecycle is managed by Counterparty Data Stewards in response to compliance events, regulatory changes, or business decisions. Transitions are immutable and audit-logged with effective dating.

Legal Entity Identifiers (LEI) are mandated for all counterparties classified as legal entities under ISO 17442. LEI is a 20-character alphanumeric code registered with GLEIF (Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation) and enables automated counterparty matching across financial markets. LEI validation is enforced at onboarding for entity_type = LEGAL_ENTITY and checked against the GLEIF Public Register for revocation. Sole proprietorships and certain government entities are exempted but require explicit exception documentation.

Know Your Customer (KYC) is a mandatory process for all active counterparties, requiring collection of beneficial ownership, funding source, and intended use of account information. KYC status is renewed annually, with kyc_expiry_date tracked and monitored. Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) is triggered for high-risk jurisdictions (FATF grey-list), Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), and customers whose business activities cannot be clearly understood. KYC exceptions require approval from the Compliance Officer.

FATCA (Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act) status classification is mandatory for all active counterparties and determines withholding tax treatment. Classifications include NRA (Non-Resident Alien), W-8 certificate holder, QI (Qualified Intermediary), and W-9 certifying entity. FATCA status is verified through customer self-certification and periodic confirmation, with misclassification audits conducted quarterly by Tax Compliance.

Counterparty records support flexible extension attributes through MongoDB, allowing business units to capture domain-specific metadata (e.g., credit tier, trading limit, preferred settlement currency) without schema changes. Extensions are versioned and linked to the SQL Server master record by entity code.

title: Client Account Data Model
html_body:
ClientAccount represents customer accounts opened with the firm, enabling trading, settlement, and custody relationships. Each account is uniquely identified within a counterparty context and specifies the account type, currency denomination, custodian relationship, and applicable regulatory regimes. ClientAccount records are the transactional anchor for all customer-facing operations.

Account types define the segregation and protection characteristics: OMNIBUS accounts pool assets from multiple beneficial owners (common in buy-side services), SEGREGATED accounts maintain per-client asset isolation (required by MiFID II for most European retail and professional clients), and PROPRIETARY accounts hold the firm's own capital. Account type determines regulatory reporting requirements, client asset protection rules, and internal approval workflows. Segregation disclosure requirements apply to omnibus accounts, requiring explicit client consent and documentation.

Currency denomination specifies the base currency for account balances, reporting, and settlement instructions. ClientAccount supports multi-currency sub-accounts through extension attributes, but the master account maintains a single primary currency. Currency must be a valid ISO 4217 code and linked to the Currency master data entity, enabling validation of deliverability and CLS eligibility for settlement planning.

Custodian relationship identifies the entity holding assets in safekeeping. Custodians may be external financial institutions (requiring custody agreements, audit reports, and segregation verification) or internal firm-controlled custody arrangements. External custodian accounts trigger operational controls and reporting requirements within Operations Finance and require annual SLA compliance tracking.

Regulatory regime field indicates which regulatory framework governs the account: EMIR (European Markets Infrastructure Regulation) for derivatives dealers, MiFID II for investment firms, UCITS for fund management, or PROPRIETARY for firm accounts. The regime drives reporting obligations, position limits, and margin requirements. An account may be subject to multiple regimes (cross-marked in extension attributes) requiring cumulative compliance.

Account lifecycle includes onboarding (account opening and initial compliance checks), active management (ongoing KYC and regulatory monitoring), and termination (account closure with settlement of all positions). Account opening requires approval from the Counterparty relationship manager, compliance screening against sanctions lists, and confirmation of beneficial ownership. Archive date marks the effective closure date after all settlement is complete.

title: Book Master Data
html_body:
Book entity represents a ledger or journal into which trading, banking, and risk management activities are recorded. Books serve as the operational and regulatory boundary for position aggregation, limit monitoring, profit and loss attribution, and capital allocation. Each book is uniquely identified by a globally-unique book code and associated with a single legal entity.

Book type classification distinguishes trading books (customer-facing risk positions subject to market valuation and trading profit rules) from banking books (held-to-maturity loans and deposits subject to banking regulations and credit risk). This distinction is fundamental to regulatory capital calculation under FRTB (Fundamental Review of Trading Book) and Banking Book regulations. The boundary between trading and banking books is defined daily by the Risk Management team, with movements requiring explicit pre-approval and audit logging.

Asset class specifies the market risk category: Equities (stocks and stock indices), Fixed Income (bonds and credit derivatives), Foreign Exchange (FX spot, forwards, and options), Commodities (energy, metals, and agricultural), Credit (credit default swaps, bond indices), and Rates (interest rate derivatives and bonds). Asset class drives valuation methodology, margin calculation, and risk reporting. A single book may manage multiple asset classes but typically does not (to maintain organizational clarity and risk appetite targeting).

Booking system field identifies the operational system where trades are recorded: MUREX (Murex Trading system for derivatives and cash products), JETBRIDGE (proprietary FX booking system), SUMMIT (risk aggregation platform), or LEGACY (retired systems requiring gradual data migration). System identification is critical for position reconciliation, regulatory reporting extract preparation, and incident response workflows.

Book limits define maximum net exposures or notional amounts that may be outstanding in the book, typically specified in USD equivalent. Limit currency is configurable and used for daily position reporting. Limit breach alerts are generated automatically and trigger escalation workflows to Risk Management leadership. Limits are reviewed quarterly by the Risk Appetite Committee and effective-dated for regulatory change tracking.

Regulatory regime flags indicate which regulatory frameworks apply: FRTB_TRADING (Fundamental Review of Trading Book under Basel III Endgame), CVA (Counterparty Valuation Adjustment under regulatory capital), EMIR (derivatives position reporting), or internal limit frameworks. A book is always subject to at least one regime, with capital charge calculations reflecting the regime weight. Books archived due to business consolidation or product exit retain historical data but prohibit new position booking.

title: Settlement Instructions (SSI)
html_body:
Settlement Instruction (SSI) entity encodes the instructions for transferring securities and/or cash between counterparties at settlement. SSI ensures that agreed transaction terms (who pays whom, what currency, what date) translate reliably into payment and delivery messaging. Each SSI is linked to a counterparty and client account and maintains the SWIFT details necessary for processing.

Settlement instruction type defines the settlement model: DVP (Delivery Versus Payment) ensures simultaneous transfer of securities for cash, eliminating counterparty credit risk at settlement. FOP (Free of Payment) transfers securities without concurrent cash payment, typically used for corporate actions or internal transfers. RVP (Receive Versus Payment) applies to securities receipt transactions. DFP (Delivery Free of Payment) applies to securities delivery without concurrent cash receipt. DVP and RVP are the standard models for securities settlements and eliminate settlement risk.

Settlement currency specifies the monetary denomination for cash settlement. Settlement currency must be a valid ISO 4217 code and validated against the Currency master data to confirm deliverability and CLS eligibility. CLS (Continuous Linked Settlement) is the primary settlement system for major currencies; CLS-eligible currencies settle through CLS Bank with reduced settlement risk, while minor currencies may require alternative settlement channels (e.g., CEDEL, Euroclear national agents).

SWIFT connectivity identifies the messaging route: swift_bic (Bank Identifier Code, 8 or 11 characters per ISO 9362) and IBAN (International Bank Account Number, up to 34 characters per ISO 13616) together enable SWIFT message routing and fund transfer initiation. BIC validation is mandatory for DVP and RVP instructions; IBAN validation is required for instruction types that involve cash movement. SWIFT message types MT103 (single customer credit transfer) and MT202 (general financial institution transfer) are used for settlement execution.

Clearing house designation identifies the central counterparty (CCP) or clearing organization: CLS (for major currency pairs), DTCC (Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation for US securities), or Euroclear (for European securities). Clearing house membership requires active standing and verified participant codes; settlement fails through non-member clearing houses are blocked and escalated to Operations.

SSI records support multiple settlement instructions per counterparty-account pair, allowing different banks, currencies, and clearing routes to be maintained. The active SSI flag indicates the instruction that will be used by default for transaction settlement; other SSIs are retained for historical audit and manual override scenarios. SSI changes require trade affirmation confirmation to ensure alignment with counterparty expectations.

title: Country Reference Data
html_body:
Country entity maintains geopolitical reference data and regulatory overlays for every country in which the firm operates. Country records capture ISO standard codes (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 and alpha-3), FATF (Financial Action Task Force) risk ratings, sanctions screening status, and operational configuration data. Country is a dimension entity used to validate counterparty jurisdiction, apply regulatory rules, and enforce sanctions compliance.

ISO 3166-1 country codes are the international standard: alpha-2 codes are 2 uppercase letters (e.g., 'GB', 'US', 'JP'), and alpha-3 codes are 3 uppercase letters. Alpha-2 is the most commonly used code in SWIFT messaging, while alpha-3 is standard in ISO reports and some reference data systems. The firm maintains both codes in the Country master and validates trading counterparties' incorporation, residence, and beneficial ownership countries against the Country master.

FATF risk status classifies countries on the FATF grey list (under monitoring for deficiencies in anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing controls), the FATF blacklist (actively non-cooperative), or white list (compliant). Grey-list countries trigger Enhanced Due Diligence for all new counterparties; blacklist countries prohibit direct business engagement under most regulatory regimes. FATF ratings are updated quarterly in response to FATF plenary decisions and shared regulatory intelligence.

Sanctions status field indicates whether a country is subject to comprehensive economic sanctions (which may affect transaction feasibility), sectoral sanctions (restrictions on specific industries or entity types), or no sanctions. The firm maintains integration with OFAC (Office of Foreign Assets Control) and EU sanctions lists; automated screening is applied at transaction processing to block prohibited trades. Country sanctions status is reviewed and updated daily in response to government proclamations.

OECD membership status indicates whether a country participates in the OECD and commits to international standards. OECD membership affects tax treaty access, withholding rates, and CRS (Common Reporting Standard) compliance obligations. Non-OECD countries may require higher FATCA and CRS documentation thresholds.

Country records support operational configuration attributes (time zone, currency of official settlement, primary banking centers) through extension attributes in MongoDB. These attributes enable system-level optimization and client asset positioning decisions. Historical country data (e.g., country code changes during geopolitical events) is retained for audit trail and restatement management.

title: Currency Reference Data
html_body:
Currency entity encodes monetary denomination metadata conforming to ISO 4217, the international standard for currency codes. Currency records are used to validate account currencies, settlement currencies, pricing currencies, and limit calculation denominators. Each currency has a 3-uppercase-letter ISO 4217 code (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP, JPY) and metadata about deliverability and settlement mechanics.

Decimal places (scale) specifies the number of fractional digits in standard pricing; for example, USD has 2 decimal places (cents), while JPY has 0 (no fractional unit). This is used by the pricing engine to format and round prices and is validated against the ISO 4217 standard to prevent data entry errors. Major currency pairs (USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, CHF, CAD, AUD, NZD) have standard scales; emerging market currencies may have variable scales.

Deliverable vs non-deliverable (NDF) flag indicates whether a currency may be freely exchanged for physical settlement. Major currencies (USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, CHF) are fully deliverable; many emerging market currencies are non-deliverable due to capital controls, requiring all settlement to occur in an alternative currency (typically USD). The isDeliverable flag is used by the FX desk to determine settlement currency for NDFs and affects counterparty credit exposure calculation.

CLS eligibility indicates whether a currency is settled through CLS Bank (Continuous Linked Settlement). CLS currencies (major pairs plus a growing list of emerging market currencies) benefit from DVP settlement and reduced settlement risk; non-CLS currencies require bilateral settlement arrangements and carry settlement risk. CLS eligibility changes as new currencies and CCPs are onboarded; eligibility updates are monitored weekly from the CLS service.

Currency status field indicates ACTIVE (in general use), LEGACY (phased out but still in use for historical positions, e.g., pre-EUR currencies), or DEPRECATED (no new accounts may be opened, existing positions must be closed or converted). The firm phases out currencies due to geopolitical events (e.g., Russia rouble, Iran rial) or economic restructuring; status transitions are coordinated with Compliance and Risk Management to ensure orderly exit.

Currency records support operational attributes through extension attributes: central bank, major settlement banks, settlement hours (e.g., FEDWIRE 21:00–22:00 UTC for USD), and pricing sources (e.g., Bloomberg, Reuters). These attributes are used by trading operations and settlement teams for operational decision-making and contingency planning. Currency master updates are reviewed daily and changes are logged with regulatory justification.

title: Entity Relationships
html_body:
Entity Relationships define the logical dependencies and composition structure of the RMDM data model. The primary relationship hierarchy flows from Counterparty down to ClientAccount, Book, and SettlementInstruction, with Country and Currency serving as reference dimensions for validation and operational configuration. Understanding relationship cardinality and constraints is essential for data quality and compliance rule enforcement.

Counterparty is the root entity; every legal entity that engages with the firm must exist as a counterparty record with ACTIVE or PROSPECT status. A counterparty may have multiple client accounts (1:N), reflecting different trading relationships, currencies, or regulatory regimes. A counterparty must be ACTIVE before new client accounts may be opened (enforced by the CACC-003 mandatory rule).

ClientAccount is a child of Counterparty; each account belongs to exactly one counterparty and specifies a unique account number within that counterparty. A client account may have multiple settlement instructions (1:N) to accommodate multiple banks or clearing houses. Account currency must reference a valid Currency entity, and any account with regulatory regime EMIR must reference a valid Counterparty with EMIR eligibility (enforced by CACC-005 rule).

Book is a child of Counterparty (via legal entity reference); a legal entity may operate multiple books (1:N) for different asset classes or regulatory boundaries. A book may NOT have zero legal entity (violates BOOK-002); a book may NOT be associated with a counterparty in TERMINATED status. Book asset class must reference valid Asset Class enumerations; book currency limits must reference Currency master data.

SettlementInstruction is a child of Counterparty and ClientAccount; the instruction specifies settlement mechanics for transactions in that account. Settlement currency must reference a valid Currency entity and be validated for CLS eligibility (rule SSIN-005). Counterparty referenced in the SSI must have status ACTIVE (rule SSIN-003). BIC/IBAN fields are validated against SWIFT standards; missing BIC/IBAN in DVP/RVP instructions are flagged by rule SSIN-004.

Country and Currency are reference entities with no direct ownership relationship; they are referenced by Counterparty (incorporation country), ClientAccount (currency), SettlementInstruction (settlement currency), and Book (limit currency). A Country entity may be referenced by multiple counterparties (M:N); a Currency entity may be referenced by multiple accounts and instructions (M:N). Referential integrity is enforced by the database schema and runtime validation rules.

title: KYC and Counterparty Onboarding
html_body:
Know Your Customer (KYC) is the foundational compliance process for counterparty onboarding and periodic verification. KYC encompasses identity verification (matching counterparty information against government ID databases), beneficial ownership identification (disclosing any individual(s) controlling ≥25% of the entity), and source of funds verification (confirming the legitimacy of customer capital). KYC is mandatory for all counterparties transitioning from PROSPECT to ACTIVE status.

Beneficial ownership identification requires disclosure of all individuals and entities with ≥25% direct or indirect control. For corporate entities, this includes shareholders, parent companies, and controlling family members. For trusts, KYC must identify the trustee, settlor, and principal beneficiaries. PEPs (Politically Exposed Persons) and their close relatives are flagged for Enhanced Due Diligence; the firm maintains a real-time integration with Thomson Reuters World-Check for automated PEP screening.

Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) is required for high-risk counterparties identified through risk factors: counterparties incorporated in FATF grey-list countries, Politically Exposed Persons, customers whose business activities are unclear, high-value customers (>USD 10M annual transaction volume), and counterparties in high-risk sectors (sanctions evasion, money laundering, proliferation of weapons of mass destruction). EDD includes source of funds verification, beneficial ownership documentation, regulatory sanctions screening, and ongoing transaction monitoring.

KYC renewal is scheduled annually; kyc_expiry_date in the Counterparty record triggers automated alerts 30 days prior to expiration. Compliance staff initiate the renewal process (requesting updated certifications and beneficial ownership confirmations); if renewal is not completed before expiration, the counterparty status is automatically downgraded from ACTIVE to SUSPENDED, blocking new trading and settlement activity. KYC lapses are logged as compliance violations and escalated to the Chief Compliance Officer.

KYC documents and certifications are retained in a document management system with cryptographic integrity verification. Scans of government ID, articles of incorporation, beneficial ownership certifications, and FATCA/CRS self-certifications are cross-referenced by customer ID and expiration date. Document retention policies are set to 7 years post-customer termination, aligned with regulatory recordkeeping requirements.

Onboarding exceptions (accepting a PROSPECT counterparty without completed KYC for a limited period) require explicit approval from the Chief Compliance Officer or delegated authority, with documented justification and a maximum 30-day exception period. Exceptions are logged as control violations and reviewed monthly by the Compliance Committee; exceptions aging past 30 days are automatically escalated and force-close the counterparty if not approved by management.

title: Data Quality Rules Framework
html_body:
Data Quality Rules enforce business requirements at the entity attribute level, ensuring that only valid and complete data flows through downstream systems. Rules are categorized as MANDATORY (non-negotiable requirements that block data propagation on violation) or CONDITIONAL (requirements that apply only when specific conditions are met). Each rule is associated with a severity level (ERROR, WARNING, INFO) that determines the enforcement action taken when violated.

MANDATORY rules encode fundamental requirements that apply to all instances of an entity. Examples include LEI format validation for legal entity counterparties, ISO 4217 currency code validation, and SWIFT BIC format verification for settlement instructions. Mandatory rule violations result in ERROR severity, preventing the record from reaching downstream systems (trading, settlement, regulatory reporting) until remediation is complete. Mandatory rules cannot be waived or overridden.

CONDITIONAL rules enforce requirements that apply when specific attribute conditions are met. Examples include: if counterparty type is LEGAL_ENTITY, then LEI is mandatory (CNTP-005); if account type is OMNIBUS, then segregation disclosure is required (CACC-004); if counterparty is incorporated in a FATF high-risk country, then Enhanced Due Diligence is triggered (CNTP-006). Conditional rules may be waived with documented justification and management approval.

Severity hierarchy determines the enforcement action: ERROR blocks downstream processing and triggers alerts to Data Stewards; WARNING allows processing to continue but generates audit logs and may trigger manual review workflows; INFO is purely informational and used for compliance audit trails without operational impact. The rule framework is designed so that most violations are detectable at data entry time, reducing downstream rework and regulatory exposure.

Rule ownership assigns accountability: each rule is owned by a specific function (Data Governance, Compliance, Risk Management, Operations). The rule owner is responsible for documenting the business justification (regulatory reference, operational requirement, or strategic decision), maintaining the rule definition through business changes, and reviewing rule effectiveness quarterly. Rules without current owners are flagged as orphaned and escalated for assignment.

Rule versioning and audit trails track rule definition changes. When a rule is modified (conditions added, severity adjusted, description refined), the previous version is archived with an effective date transition. This allows historical compliance validation: when regulators ask 'was this record compliant as of January 2024?', the system can re-evaluate the record using the rule definitions that were in effect on that date.

title: Critical Data Elements Policy
html_body:
Critical Data Elements (CDEs) are data attributes that, if inaccurate or incomplete, would result in regulatory risk, operational failure, or financial loss. CDE designation is a governance decision that triggers heightened validation, monitoring, and audit requirements. The firm's CDE policy is aligned with BCBS 239 (Principles for effective governance of technology in banking organisations) and applies governance disciplines across all three RMDM core functions: Data Governance, Data Stewardship, and Data Architecture.

CDE examples across entities include: Counterparty LEI (regulatory reporting, EMIR, sanctions screening), ClientAccount currency (settlement calculation, FX exposure), Book code (position tracking, regulatory reporting), Book trading status (limit enforcement, risk aggregation), SettlementInstruction BIC/IBAN (payment execution), and Currency CLS eligibility (settlement planning). A comprehensive CDE inventory is maintained in the RMDM data dictionary and reviewed annually for completeness and relevance.

Data owners assign CDE designation to attributes, establishing data quality SLAs: accuracy (data matches the external source of truth), completeness (data is populated for all applicable records), timeliness (data is current within specified refresh intervals), and validity (data conforms to defined format/enumeration rules). CDE accuracy is monitored continuously through automated reconciliation against source systems; accuracy metrics are published weekly in the Data Quality dashboard.

CDE data quality dimensions are measured and reported: Accuracy (% of records matching authoritative source), Completeness (% of records with non-null values for CDE attributes), Timeliness (% of records updated within SLA), and Validity (% of records passing format/enumeration validation). Each dimension has a target service level (typically 99% for accuracy and completeness) and remediation workflows when metrics fall below target.

BCBS 239 governance applies to CDEs: executive accountability (a senior business executive owns each CDE), change control (any CDE definition change requires governance committee approval), periodic validation (CDEs are validated against source systems monthly), and incident management (CDE errors are logged as incidents with root cause analysis and corrective action plans). CDE governance metrics are reported to the Board Risk Committee quarterly.

CDE remediation processes are codified and automated where possible. When a CDE record fails validation (e.g., a counterparty LEI fails GLEIF verification), an automated alert is generated to the Data Steward, triggering investigation and correction. If the record cannot be corrected within the SLA, the record is escalated to the Data Owner and business line leadership, with potential trading restrictions applied to affected customer accounts.

title: System Integration Map
html_body:
System Integration Map documents the source of truth and data flow for each RMDM entity and attribute. The map specifies which system holds the authoritative master record, which systems subscribe to that data, the refresh frequency, and the reconciliation procedure. Integration ownership is assigned to technology and operations teams; integration failures are escalated to operational leadership.

Counterparty master data is sourced from multiple systems depending on counterparty type and role. Legal entity counterparties are primarily sourced from Bloomberg (via daily data feed) for large financial institutions and Morningstar for smaller entities. Beneficial ownership and PEP status are sourced from Thomson Reuters World-Check. Client relationship management systems (Salesforce or equivalent) are the source of prospect counterparties pending onboarding. RMDM serves as the federation point, reconciling feeds and maintaining the single source of truth.

ClientAccount master data is maintained by the firm's account opening system (typically the online banking platform or account management system). ClientAccount records are created at account opening and updated through change requests (account closure, currency change, custodian change). RMDM subscribes to these updates and validates them against business rules before propagating to downstream settlement and risk systems. Orphaned accounts (no active counterparty) are flagged and escalated to Operations Finance.

Book master data is sourced from booking systems (Murex, JetBridge, or legacy platforms). Book creation in the booking system triggers a book registration request to RMDM, which validates legal entity and asset class references. Book master changes (status transition, asset class change, limit change) are synchronized via event streams or periodic batch reconciliation. Trading books are marked read-only in RMDM after transition to ARCHIVED status.

Settlement Instruction master data is maintained by the settlement operations system or the firm's payment messaging system. SSI changes (bank change, currency change) are initiated through a request workflow in the settlement system, validated against SWIFT standards, and propagated to RMDM for compliance rule checking. Critical SSI errors (invalid BIC, missing IBAN for DVP) are flagged immediately and prevent settlement of affected trades.

Country and Currency reference data are sourced from Bloomberg, ISO registry feeds, and regulatory datasets (FATF, OFAC, EU sanctions). Country updates are monthly and include FATF status changes, sanctions updates, and ISO code corrections. Currency updates are monthly and include CLS eligibility changes and deprecation announcements. Reference data updates are applied in batch overnight; any data quality issues are investigated the next business morning.

title: EMIR Reporting Reference Data
html_body:
EMIR (European Market Infrastructure Regulation) is a regulatory framework governing the central clearing and reporting of Over-The-Counter (OTC) derivatives. EMIR requires that derivative trades be reported to trade repositories (TRs) within specific timeframes, with comprehensive reference and transaction data. RMDM maintains EMIR-specific counterparty attributes and supports the enrichment of transaction data with EMIR-required fields.

Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) is the primary EMIR-required field for counterparty identification on trade reports. EMIR mandates 20-character ISO 17442 LEI for all entities involved in derivatives trading, enabling EMIR data aggregation and systemic risk assessment by financial regulators. LEI is validated against the GLEIF Public Register; revoked or superseded LEIs are flagged and must be updated before new trades are reported.

EMIR status flag in Counterparty indicates whether a counterparty is subject to EMIR obligations (EMIR dealer, counterparty to EMIR-covered trades) or exempt (end-user exemption, clearing member, etc.). EMIR status is determined by regulatory guidance and business classification; status misclassification results in reporting violations. Quarterly validation reconciles EMIR status against regulatory databases and business line confirmations.

Unique Trade Identifier (UTI) is a data model element describing the identifier assigned to each derivative trade. UTI must be unique per reporting counterparty pair per day and is carried forward throughout the trade lifecycle (execution, allocation, amendment, termination). RMDM does not store UTI (which is transactional and generated by booking systems), but references the UTI schema to support trade report generation.

Entity classification flags are required for EMIR reporting: FC (Financial Counterparty) for dealers and large asset managers; NFC+ (Non-Financial Counterparty Plus) for non-financial entities exceeding the EMIR derivative trading threshold; NFC- (Non-Financial Counterparty Minus) for smaller non-financial entities; and CCPs (Central Counterparties) cleared trades. Classification determines clearing and margining obligations; misclassification results in breach of regulatory requirements.

Trade Repository (TR) connectivity attributes identify the TR(s) to which trades are reported. The firm may use DTCC TIW (Trade Information Warehouse) or other registered TRs; RMDM maintains TR assignment by counterparty. Trade reporting job failures (malformed XML, invalid UTI, missing LEI) are tracked daily and escalated to Compliance and Operations to ensure timely regulatory reporting.

title: FATCA and Tax Compliance
html_body:
FATCA (Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act) is U.S. tax legislation requiring financial institutions to identify U.S. tax-resident customers and report their financial accounts to the U.S. Internal Revenue Service (IRS). FATCA compliance requires collection of W-8 or W-9 forms, GIIN (Global Intermediary Identification Number) verification, and proper withholding tax treatment. RMDM maintains FATCA status and documentation for all counterparties.

FATCA entity classification determines withholding requirements: NRA (Non-Resident Alien) individuals withholding 30% unless treaty protected, QI (Qualified Intermediary) agents providing pass-through reporting without local withholding, W-8 certificate holders asserting non-U.S. status, and W-9 certificate holders self-identifying as U.S. persons subject to U.S. tax. Classification is supported by self-certifications (W-8BEN, W-8BEN-E for foreign entities, W-9 for U.S. persons) and verified by Tax Compliance annually.

GIIN (Global Intermediary Identification Number) is assigned by the IRS to financial institutions participating in FATCA. If the firm qualifies as a Qualified Intermediary (QI), its GIIN is required on Form W-8IMY submitted by agent counterparties; QI status is verified through the IRS GIIN lookup tool. FATCA non-compliance results in penalties of 30% withholding on all U.S.-source income, which is operationally complex to reverse.

CRS (Common Reporting Standard) is the OECD global equivalent of FATCA, requiring reporting of financial account information of non-residents to their tax authorities. CRS applies in the firm's domestic jurisdiction and affects counterparties with accounts held with the firm. CRS status is based on tax residency (determined by FATCA W-8 forms and beneficial ownership documentation), and annual CRS reports are filed with the domestic tax authority for all non-resident account holders.

Withholding tax treatment is determined by FATCA and CRS status: NRAs subject to treaty withholding (typically 15% for dividends, 0% for interest), QIs passing through withholding obligations to beneficial owners, and W-9 entities subject to U.S. income tax without withholding. Withholding rates are reviewed quarterly to reflect treaty updates and changes in tax law. Withholding tax reconciliation (withholding calculated vs. withholding remitted to tax authorities) is performed monthly.

FATCA exemptions and exceptions may apply for certain entity types (governments, central banks, international organizations, retirement plans). Exempt entities require special classification and are typically excluded from FATCA reporting; exemption claims require documentary support (e.g., government certification, certificate of exempt status) retained for audit. Exempt status is reviewed annually and automatically expires unless renewed, defaulting to standard FATCA treatment if not updated.

title: Data Governance Operating Model
html_body:
Data Governance Operating Model defines the roles, responsibilities, and decision-making authority for RMDM throughout the data lifecycle. The operating model is organized around three core functions: Data Ownership (business accountability for data quality and regulatory compliance), Data Stewardship (operational responsibility for data maintenance and issue resolution), and Data Architecture (technology design and system integration). Each function has defined roles and escalation paths.

Data Owner role is assigned to a senior business executive (typically a business line head or function leader) and carries accountability for data quality, regulatory compliance, and business value. Data owners define data quality SLAs, approve exceptions to data quality rules, and sponsor system investments to improve data capabilities. Data owner accountability is contractual; data quality gaps and regulatory violations are escalated to CFO and Chief Compliance Officer with data owner-led remediation plans.

Data Steward role is assigned to experienced operational staff and carries responsibility for data accuracy, completeness, and timeliness. Data stewards perform daily data quality monitoring, investigate data discrepancies, and execute remediation workflows (data correction, exception approval, escalation). Steward teams are organized by entity type (Counterparty stewards, Settlement stewards, Reference data stewards); stewards report to data owners and technology leadership.

Data Architecture function owns the system design, data integration, and technical controls. Data Architects design RMDM schema, implement validation rules and reconciliation procedures, and oversee source system integration and data pipeline reliability. Architects report to the CIO and Chief Technology Officer and interface with business stakeholders through data owners.

Data Governance Committee is the executive decision body for RMDM policies, rule changes, and strategic direction. The committee meets monthly and includes representatives from Finance, Risk, Compliance, Operations, and Technology. Committee decisions (new CDE designation, rule modifications, system investments) require consensus and are ratified through formal governance documentation. Committee meeting minutes are retained for regulatory audit and Sarbanes-Oxley compliance documentation.

Data quality SLAs define required performance: counterparty data 99% accuracy (daily reconciliation), client accounts 99% completeness (all accounts populated), settlement instructions 99.5% validity (all BIC/IBAN valid), and reference data 100% currency (updated within 24 hours of source change). SLA performance is measured and reported weekly to data owners; SLA breaches trigger root cause analysis and escalation to leadership. Chronic SLA failures may result in system investment approvals or process redesigns.

title: UK Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) Reference Data
html_body:
Overview

The UK Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) 2007 is the official system for categorising business activities, maintained by the Office for National Statistics (ONS). It is the primary classification used for regulatory reporting in the United Kingdom and aligns with international standards. All counterparties and locations registered in the RMDM system are assigned a SIC code reflecting their primary business activity.

Hierarchy

SIC 2007 follows a five-level hierarchical structure:

  • Section — single letter (A through U), the broadest category
  • Division — 2-digit numeric code (e.g., 64)
  • Group — 3-digit numeric code (e.g., 641)
  • Class — 4-digit numeric code (e.g., 6419)
  • Subclass — 5-digit numeric code (e.g., 64191), the most granular level used for assignment

Section K: Financial and Insurance Activities

Section K covers divisions 64, 65, and 66 and encompasses the full range of financial intermediation, insurance, and auxiliary financial services:

DivisionDescription
64Financial service activities, except insurance and pension funding
65Insurance, reinsurance and pension funding, except compulsory social security
66Activities auxiliary to financial services and insurance activities

Key SIC Codes in Financial Services

SIC CodeDescription
64191Banks — monetary intermediation by deposit-taking institutions other than the central bank
64301Investment trusts — activities of closed-end collective investment undertakings
64910Financial leasing
65201Life insurance — direct insurance business
66190Activities auxiliary to financial services, not elsewhere classified
66300Fund management activities — management of regulated and unregulated collective investment schemes

Governance

The ONS is the statutory custodian of the UK SIC list and publishes updates following significant economic structural changes. Financial institutions are required to use the official ONS-published SIC codes on regulatory returns submitted to the Bank of England, FCA, and Companies House. The RMDM team monitors ONS announcements and applies SIC code updates to the reference data master within five business days of publication.

Usage in the MDM System

SIC codes are assigned to two key entities in RMDM: Counterparty (reflecting the legal entity's primary business activity for regulatory reporting and risk classification) and Location (reflecting the activity conducted at that premises). SIC codes are used in Bank of England statistical returns, FCA registration, and operational risk categorisation. Both 4-digit Class and 5-digit Subclass codes are stored, enabling reports at varying levels of granularity.

Cross-Reference to NACE Rev.2

UK SIC 2007 is broadly aligned with the EU's NACE Rev.2 classification but introduces UK-specific subclasses (the 5th digit) to capture distinctions not made in the European framework. Mapping tables between SIC 2007 and NACE Rev.2 are maintained in the RMDM reference data catalogue and referenced when producing EU regulatory submissions (EMIR, MiFID II) alongside UK filings.

title: NACE Rev.2 Economic Activity Classification
html_body:
Overview

NACE (Nomenclature statistique des Activités économiques dans la Communauté Européenne) Rev.2 is the EU standard statistical classification of economic activities, maintained by Eurostat. It is the reference classification for EU regulatory reporting under frameworks such as EMIR, MiFID II, Solvency II, and EBA guidelines. Financial institutions operating across EU member states use NACE codes to classify counterparties and business activities consistently across jurisdictions.

Hierarchy

NACE Rev.2 uses a four-level hierarchy:

  • Section — single letter (A through U)
  • Division — 2-digit code (e.g., 64)
  • Group — dot-separated 3-digit format (e.g., 64.1)
  • Class — dot-separated 4-digit format (e.g., 64.19), the most granular NACE level

Unlike UK SIC 2007, NACE does not have a 5th-digit subclass layer; EU member states may add national subclasses below NACE Class level.

Section K: Financial and Insurance Activities

NACE Section K covers financial intermediation, insurance, and auxiliary activities:

CodeDescription
K64Financial service activities, except insurance and pension funding
K64.1Monetary intermediation
K64.11Central banking
K64.19Other monetary intermediation (commercial banks, savings banks)
K64.3Trusts, funds and similar financial entities
K65Insurance, reinsurance and pension funding
K66Activities auxiliary to financial services and insurance
K66.1Activities auxiliary to financial services, except insurance and pension funding
K66.30Fund management activities

Mapping to UK SIC 2007

NACE K64.19 (Other monetary intermediation) maps broadly to UK SIC 64191 (Banks). NACE K66.30 (Fund management activities) maps to UK SIC 66300. Where NACE classes are broader than UK SIC subclasses, the RMDM mapping table records a one-to-many relationship. This bidirectional mapping is used when preparing cross-border regulatory submissions that reference both classification systems.

Governance

Eurostat is the authoritative custodian of NACE Rev.2. The ECB, EBA, and ESMA reference NACE codes in their reporting technical standards. NACE codes are updated through the EU comitology process; changes are typically adopted into UK MDM systems for EU reporting continuity even post-Brexit. The RMDM governance team reviews Eurostat updates quarterly and updates the NACE reference master accordingly.

Usage in MDM

NACE codes complement UK SIC codes in RMDM and are held as a parallel attribute on Counterparty and Location entities. NACE is the mandatory classification for EMIR trade repository reporting, MiFID II transaction reporting to ESMA, and EBA supervisory reporting formats (COREP, FINREP). Counterparties without a valid NACE code cannot be marked as EMIR-eligible in the RMDM system.

title: UK Regional Reference Data (ITL1 Regions)
html_body:
Overview

The ONS International Territorial Level 1 (ITL1) framework divides the United Kingdom into 12 statistical regions used for geographic analysis, regulatory reporting, and economic statistics. ITL1 regions are the primary geographic dimension assigned to Location entities in the RMDM system, enabling geographic risk aggregation, regulatory geographic returns, and operational coverage analysis.

The 12 ITL1 Regions

ITL1 CodeRegion NameCountry
TLCLondonEngland
TLDSouth EastEngland
TLEEast of EnglandEngland
TLFSouth WestEngland
TLGWest MidlandsEngland
TLHEast MidlandsEngland
TLIYorkshire and The HumberEngland
TLJNorth WestEngland
TLKNorth EastEngland
TLLScotlandScotland
TLMWalesWales
TLNNorthern IrelandNorthern Ireland

History and Governance

The ITL framework replaced the NUTS (Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics) classification in January 2021 following the UK's departure from the European Union. NUTS was the EU standard administered by Eurostat; ITL is the UK-domestic equivalent administered by the ONS. The ONS serves as the statutory custodian of ITL codes and boundary definitions; code changes are rare but possible following significant local government reorganisations or devolution changes.

ITL codes at levels 2 and 3 provide finer geographic granularity beneath ITL1 regions (analogous to NUTS 2 and NUTS 3). RMDM stores ITL1 codes as the standard geographic attribute for Locations; finer-grained ITL2 and ITL3 codes may be stored as extension attributes when required for specific reporting obligations.

Usage in MDM

Region codes are assigned to Location entities in RMDM and serve three primary purposes:

  • Geographic risk aggregation — operational risk, concentration risk, and business continuity planning use ITL1 regions to identify geographic exposure
  • Regulatory reporting — Bank of England statistical returns, ONS business surveys, and HMRC filings reference ITL1 region codes for geographic breakdown
  • Operational coverage analysis — the business uses ITL1 regions to track branch network coverage, staffing distribution, and service availability

Relationship to Country

All 12 ITL1 regions belong to the United Kingdom, represented by ISO 3166-1 country code GB. The RMDM system stores Country as a separate reference dimension; Region is a sub-national classification beneath Country. Foreign locations (outside the UK) do not carry ITL1 codes; their geographic classification uses the Country entity and ISO 3166-2 subdivision codes where applicable.

title: Location Master Data
html_body:
Overview

The Location entity represents a physical business premises at which the firm or a counterparty conducts operations. Location master data captures full address, geographic classification, business type, operational status, and industry classification codes. It is a core reference entity in RMDM, linked to both Counterparty (to describe where a counterparty operates) and Region/Country for geographic risk reporting.

Data Model

FieldDescriptionFormat / Example
LocationIdUnique identifier for the location recordLOC001, LOC042
LocationNameDescriptive name of the premisesLondon Head Office
AddressLine1First line of the registered address25 Canada Square
CityCity or townLondon
PostcodeUK postcode (validated against Royal Mail PAF)E14 5LQ
RegionIdFK to ITL1 Region (see UK Regional Reference Data)TLC (London)
CountryISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codeGB
BusinessTypeClassification of the operational role of the premisesHead Office
StatusOperational lifecycle statusActive
SicCodeUK SIC 2007 subclass code (5-digit)64191
NaceCodeNACE Rev.2 class codeK64.19

BusinessType Enumeration

The BusinessType field uses a controlled enumeration to classify the operational role of each location:

  • Head Office — primary registered office and principal place of business
  • Branch — a regulated branch of a licensed entity
  • Regional Office — a regional management centre without full branch authorisation
  • Trading Office — a location where trading activities are conducted
  • Back Office — an operations and support centre
  • Data Centre — a technology infrastructure facility
  • Operations Centre — a processing and operations hub
  • Service Centre — a client-facing service and support location

Status Lifecycle

Location records follow a three-stage lifecycle:

  • Active — the location is operational and all services are available
  • Under Review — the location is under governance review (e.g., planned closure, regulatory inspection, or address change verification)
  • Inactive — the location has ceased operations; the record is retained for audit trail and historical reporting

Status transitions from Active to Inactive require approval from the Data Governance Committee. Transitions to Under Review may be initiated by Data Stewards. No new Counterparty relationships may be created against a location with Inactive status.

SIC and NACE Classification

Each location is assigned both a UK SIC 2007 code (5-digit subclass) and a NACE Rev.2 code (4-digit class) indicating its primary business activity. These codes may differ from the parent Counterparty's SIC/NACE codes where the location conducts a specialist function (e.g., a back-office operations centre might carry SIC 82990 while its parent counterparty carries SIC 64191). SIC and NACE codes on locations are validated against the respective reference data masters and must belong to Section K (financial services) or adjacent sections as defined by the firm's business scope policy.

Governance

Location master data is owned by the Operations Data Owner. Any change to a location's status, address, or BusinessType requires a change request submitted through the RMDM governance portal and approved by the Data Governance Committee. Address changes are verified against Royal Mail Postcode Address File (PAF) before approval. SIC and NACE code changes require documented justification referencing the ONS and Eurostat classification standards.

Relationships to Other Entities

A Location is associated with one or more Counterparty records, reflecting the premises where a counterparty's operations are based. A single Counterparty may maintain multiple Locations (e.g., Head Office, Branch, Back Office). Each Location is classified by exactly one Region (ITL1), one Country, one SicCode, and one NaceCode. Location data feeds into geographic risk aggregation, Bank of England geographic returns, business continuity planning registers, and operational risk mapping exercises.
